explanation_used:
1. **Opening statement**: This neuron strongly responds to **possessive time references indicating recent or current events** (such as "this week’s Friday Night Live," "Tuesday’s speech from the throne," "last year’s flooding," or "today’s teens").

2. **Domain coverage**: The pattern appears predominantly in English-language news updates, event announcements, and current-affairs reporting. It spans topics like politics, entertainment, sports, and community events, often in contexts that emphasize timeliness (e.g., "this week’s releases," "Monday night's game," "this year’s competition").

3. **Contrast with low-activation examples**: The high-activation texts consistently contain possessive time phrases ("this week’s," "today’s," "this year’s") that anchor the content to a specific, often recent, timeframe. The low-activation examples lack such constructions; they include non-English text, general statements without temporal specificity, or references to past/future events without possessive time framing (e.g., "For 2023’s Martin Luther King Jr. Day").

4. **Closing interpretation**: The neuron detects linguistic constructions that highlight immediacy or temporal relevance, specifically possessive noun phrases where a time period owns or defines a related event. It acts as a marker for text segments emphasizing current updates or recently anchored information.

results.actual_text: very early universe. This was completely unexpected from previous data. The only other class of supermassive black holes we knew about in the very early universe are quas
results.plain_text: New discovery sheds light on very early supermassive black holes  Astronomers from the University of Texas and the University of Arizona have discovered a rapidly growing black hole in one of the most extreme galaxies known in the very early universe. The discovery of the galaxy and the black hole at its center provides new clues on the formation of the very first supermassive black holes. The new work is published in Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society.   Using observations taken with the Atacama Large Millimeter Array (ALMA), a radio observatory sited in Chile, the team have determined that the galaxy, named COS-87259, containing this new supermassive black hole is very extreme, forming stars at a rate 1000 times that of our own Milky Way and containing over a billion solar masses worth of interstellar dust. The galaxy shines bright from both this intense burst of star formation and the growing supermassive black hole at its center. The black hole is considered to be a new type of primordial black hole—one heavily enshrouded by cosmic "dust," causing nearly all of its light to be emitted in the mid-infrared range of the electromagnetic spectrum. The researchers have also found that this growing supermassive black hole (frequently referred to as an active galactic nucleus) is generating a strong jet of material moving at near light speed through the host galaxy. Today, black holes with masses millions to billions of times greater than that of our own sun sit at the center of nearly every galaxy. How these supermassive black holes first formed remains a mystery for scientists, particularly because several of these objects have been found when the universe was very young. Because the light from these sources takes so long to reach us, we see them as they existed in the past; in this case, just 750 million years after the Big Bang, which is approximately 5% of the current age of the universe. What is particularly astonishing about this new object is that it was identified over a relatively small patch of the sky typically used to detect similar objects—less than 10 times the size of the full moon—suggesting there could be thousands of similar sources in the very early universe. This was completely unexpected from previous data. The only other class of supermassive black holes we knew about in the very early universe are quasars, which are active black holes that are relatively unobscured by cosmic dust. These quasars are extremely rare at distances similar to COS-87259, with only a few tens located over the full sky. The surprising discovery of COS-87259 and its black hole raises several questions about the abundance of very early supermassive black holes, as well as the types of galaxies in which they typically form. Ryan Endsley, the lead author of the paper and now a Postdoctoral Fellow at The University of Texas at Austin, says, "These results suggest that very early supermassive black holes were often heavily obscured by dust, perhaps as a consequence of the intense star formation activity in their host galaxies. This is something others have been predicting for a few years now, and it's really nice to see the first direct observational evidence supporting this scenario." Similar types of objects have been found in the more local, present-day universe, such as Arp 299. In this system, two galaxies are crashing together generating an intense starburst as well as heavy obscuration of the growing supermassive black hole in one of the two galaxies. Endsley adds, "While nobody expected to find this kind of object in the very early universe, its discovery takes a step towards building a much better understanding of how billion solar mass black holes were able to form so early on in the lifetime of the universe, as well how the most massive galaxies first evolved." More information: Ryan Endsley et al, ALMA Confirmation of an Obscured Hyperluminous Radio-Loud AGN at z=6.853 Associated with a Dusty Starburst in the 1.5 deg2 COSMOS Field, Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society (2023). DOI: 10.1093/mnras/stad266 Journal information: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society  Provided by Royal Astronomical Society

results.actual_text: , about your life in Toronto. In honour of Mother’s Day, this week’s question is: What’s the best moment you’ve spent with your mom in Toronto
results.plain_text: Readers corner is an occasional series where we hear from you, our readers, about your life in Toronto. In honour of Mother’s Day, this week’s question is: What’s the best moment you’ve spent with your mom in Toronto? ‘She was so excited to be on a streetcar after so many years’ My mother grew up in Toronto but moved with her new husband to St. Catharines in 1953. Since her three daughters and all her siblings lived in Toronto, she would frequently come in by bus to visit. One day in 1999, I went to pick her up from the Bay and Dundas bus station. My husband needed the car, so Mom and I got on a crowded Dundas streetcar instead. She was so excited to be on a streetcar after so many years, and she started a conversation with a few people standing near her about how she’d grown up in Toronto but had moved away and all the wonderful memories she had of the city. I told her people in Toronto don’t start conversations with strangers in streetcars, and the few people chatting with her laughed and kept up the conversation. She was so happy leaving the streetcar; she wished her fellow travellers well, thanked the streetcar driver and talked about her “old Toronto” for the rest of the evening. — Dale Mayerson ‘A perfect trip for three generations’ My mother’s Christmas-season birthday was approaching, and I came up with the perfect gift: I would take her to see the famous beautiful ballet ‘The Nutcracker’ at the Hummingbird Theatre, along with my five-year-old daughter. A matinee performance followed by a delicious steak dinner at the Keg and a fun-filled overnight stay at the Harbour Castle — a perfect trip for three generations. Nothing is more wonderful than downtown Toronto at the peak of the Christmas season! Everything looks so enchanting and magical, like a fairyland. My mother had never before been to a ballet, and she fell in love with the music that my daughter and I knew by heart. The best part was when we were waiting for dessert at the Keg. For its presentation to my mother — the ‘birthday girl’ — three handsome young men came out to serenade her with a fabulous rendition of “She’s Young, She’s Beautiful & She’s Mine.” Suddenly, the highlight of the day was no longer the ballet but the sparkle of surprise and delight in my mother’s eyes, the blush in her cheeks and the squeal of my young daughter’s laughter at Grandma’s birthday song. Even though my mother has now been gone many years, the lingering memories of that special birthday in the “big city” still bring a smile to my face and warm my heart. — Ruth Lampkin ‘We all still love to see a live show’ My two sisters and I were raised by my mom on her own with no support. Regardless, she instilled in us the love of live theatre. I saw my very first production of “The Mouse Trap” at the Toronto Truck Theatre. It was so exciting and we all enjoyed it tremendously. It was a very big deal, and I’m sure it wasn’t easy getting three young girls down to the show by TTC. I was so proud to take my own three children to see the same play years later. We all still love to see a live show, and I love taking my mom and showing my gratitude for all that she has shared with me. — Marie Greatrix ‘That day tasted like freedom’ My single mom worked full-time during the week and often on weekends to make ends meet for me and my two sisters. We lived in Scarborough, and as a treat one weekend, she decided to take us and each of our best friends on an outing downtown. We caused quite a stir on our journey: three pairs of girls chittering on the TTC bus, paired up in seats. At first I was worried that my mom would have to sit alone, but she looked at us all scrubbed up and wearing our Sunday best like a proud mother hen with her chicks. On the subway, strangers smiled at my mom and happily moved around to let us sit together in the front car. We watched in awe as we made our way underground. We went to the Eaton Centre first. “Look up,” my mom said, and six pairs of eyes followed her arm. I think it was the first time I saw the “Flight Stop” geese. Even later, after working in the Cadillac Fairview Tower for many years, that view never got old. We walked to Nathan Phillips Square, and my sister and I climbed “The Archer.” Then Mom bought us all soft ice cream cones from a truck on Queen Street in front of City Hall. That day tasted like freedom. Any time I have ice cream from a truck, I get that same lift. My sisters and I are still best friends. I love you, Mom. — Nancy Rule ‘That very simple memory always makes me smile’ I have so many great memories of spending time with my mom. We would go to the Stratford Festival to catch a play every summer. A couple of times a year, we would take the subway downtown and spend the day in the shops on the Mink Mile looking at all the beautiful clothes, having lunch at Remys, just being together. One afternoon, we ended up at Yorkdale. I don’t remember the exact reason for the sudden burst of laughter that shook us both at the same time. We were walking side by side and had to stop because we were bent over in explosive laughter. People were hurrying past, concentrating on their destinations, involved in their own thoughts, though I think I saw a couple of strangers smile at us. That very simple memory always makes me smile and makes me a little sad thinking how much the world has changed. She is still here at 87 and we still laugh, just not as explosively as that one belly laugh etched on my memory. — Stefania Bortolus ‘The evening was one of pure enjoyment’ It was August of 1988 and a week before my marriage. My fiancée had already departed for Vancouver to oversee preparations for our wedding while I remained in Toronto to complete summer-school teaching work. The night before my own leave-taking for Vancouver, my mother and I went to see the movie “Die Hard,” with Bruce Willis, and to have dinner at a favourite restaurant near St. Clair and Yonge. The evening was one of pure enjoyment: an entertaining film, a delicious meal, a beautiful summer evening and good conversation. Though I cannot — from a distance of almost 33 years — recall exactly what we talked about, we no doubt reflected upon the past while expressing our hopes and aspirations for the future. For both mother and son, it was a moment of transition — a new page in our lives’ chapters was being turned. Loading... — Rabbi Bill S. Tepper ‘This was a treat for me’ Every year, my mother would take me downtown to see the Christmas window displays in the Eaton’s and Simpsons stores. The animated displays were always mesmerizing, especially for a wide-eyed young girl. At lunchtime we would go to the basement of the Eaton’s store to enjoy a delicious hot dog and Honeydew drink. This was a treat for me since my family didn’t eat in restaurants or food bars as a rule because my parents were very frugal, probably a result of habits formed as immigrants to Canada following World War II. — Nina Ingrey ‘We loved sitting at the counter like grown-ups’ My mom and dad immigrated to Canada in 1953 along with my older sister. I was born in 1954, and we lived in downtown Toronto on Pendrith Street (Christie and Bloor streets). It was a great community! My mom would often take us shopping at Honest Ed’s, which was always fun. Sometimes we would have to line up to get in, which would make it more exciting. Each floor had different items and was full of people and kids running around. Then she would take us across the street to the 5 & 10 store. My mom would take us over to the lunch counter and get us a hot dog and a Coke to have while she walked around the store. It was not very big, so she could see us from anywhere in the store. We loved sitting at the counter like grown-ups! My mom was always happy leaving because she would have found some great bargains and we would be happy because she would often buy us something special, too, along with our favourite lunch! Sadly, my mom passed away in 1963 from breast cancer, but walking along Bloor Street and spending the day with her is a memory that I will never forget. — Dina Simon ‘My mom had a beloved repertoire of laughs’ As a surprise for her birthday, I took my mom to see David Sedaris at Massey Hall. My mom had a beloved repertoire of laughs — from her sweet one-note giggle to her signature all-out laugh, when she covered her mouth with a fist and shrieked. It was one of the greatest sounds in my life, and a theatre full of strangers was treated to an hour of it as we laughed ourselves sick. Buzzing from the show, we stumbled out of the theatre and accidentally into Nuit Blanche. Giant, multistoreyed grotesque clown heads were smashed in between buildings, staring at us. The streets were full of people. Not a car in sight. It felt like magic. These things always happened with my mom — beauty and adventure had a way of finding her. I lost her a year and a half ago, and these memories of the two of us remind me to keep laughing when I want to cry. Especially on Mother’s Day.” — Geneviève Parent This week’s question: What is your favourite park in Toronto? Send your answers to: together@thestar.ca.

results.actual_text: at least two eligible scholarship quarterbacks. "I think it goes back to what I’ve been preaching from Day 1. In order for us to be back to Florida
results.plain_text: Florida State head coach Willie Taggart entered spring practice emphasizing a need to create competitive depth across the roster. One major spot this wasn't the case coming into FSU's spring was at quarterback. The Seminoles began spring practice with only one scholarship quarterback they were sure would be eligible in 2019 in James Blackman. The addition of Wisconsin graduate transfer Alex Hornibrook Sunday ensured that FSU will enter the season with at least two eligible scholarship quarterbacks. "I think it goes back to what I’ve been preaching from Day 1. In order for us to be back to Florida State and be where we want to be, we’ve got to make sure we have competitive depth at every position," Taggart said when addressing the media for the first time since Hornibrook committed. "It will allow us to have competitive depth at the quarterback position and to come in and compete with James. Just like every other position we have, we have some depth now." Hornibrook will arrive this summer after spending the last four seasons with the Badgers. He appeared in 35 games and led UW to an Orange Bowl victory over Miami to cap off a 13-1 2017 season. He leaves the Badgers with a 26-6 (.813) record as a starter, giving him the best winning percentage for a quarterback in program history. "You have a guy that’s played," Taggart said. "He’s played in a lot of games and he’s won big games. That helps." On the surface, Hornibrook isn't a great system fit for what Taggart and offensive coordinator Kendal Briles want to run at FSU. He's not a dual-threat quarterback -- having never run for positive yards in any of his three seasons -- and running an offense at UW that relied far more on the run than it did the pass. Still, Taggart is confident that they would be able to make the offense work for Hornibrook's skill set if it came to that. "The thing I like about it is he has a coach here who’s used different kind of quarterbacks. He’s used guys who are not necessarily a dual threat. He’s used pocket guys and he’s used dual-threat guys," Taggart said. "I think with our system, it doesn’t matter whether you’re pro style or dual threat. You should be able to operate our system if you’re smart and can throw the football." Hornibrook will have his work cut out for him upon his arrival. He'll be well behind Blackman in learning a system he's unfamiliar with and in team chemistry. While Blackman remains the clear frontrunner to land the starting job for the Seminoles' season opener against Boise State, Taggart sees the advantages of competition at the position entering fall camp. "I don’t think James came here to be afraid of competition. I don’t think you come to Florida State to be afraid of competition," Taggart said. "I think that’s why James came to Florida State. And knowing he’s going to compete is going to make him better and allow him to be the best, and knowing if he’s going to be the guy that he went against some really good guys and beat out some really good guys."  Brady Scott's versatility The FSU offensive line was a constantly changing carousel last season. Injuries and inability forced the Seminoles to use nine different offensive line combinations in their 12 games. Because of this, someone with the versatility that redshirt sophomore Brady Scott has shown during his time at FSU has been invaluable. "I can play anywhere on the line," Scott said. "Wherever coach tells me to play, I'm gonna play and I'm gonna try my best and give my best effort." Scott was viewed as a center or tackle upon his arrival before redshirting in 2017. A desperate lack of depth forced Scott to make six starts and appear in 10 games at tackle in 2018. Although not in his natural position, he proved to be the best and most consistent of FSU's poor options at the right tackle spot. Behind an offseason which has seen him put on good weight, Scott is now working with the first team offensive line at left guard this spring. "I think, for one, he's a smart young man and that's huge being up front. I think if you're not smart, it's hard to play different positions. He's smart, he's a tough kid and it's really important to him. No matter where you put him at, he wants to make coach happy," Taggart said. "He's another young man that has really put the work in this offseason, picking up some weight and getting stronger. Last year, that probably hurt him more than anything. He just wasn't ready from a physical standpoint. Now, he's gotten stronger and is more comfortable and confident in what he's doing and it's showing in our football team."

results.actual_text: DNA Exclusive: How Japan Stands Firm Amidst Earthquakes? In tonight's
results.plain_text: DNA Exclusive: How Japan Stands Firm Amidst Earthquakes? In tonight's edition of DNA, Zee News prime time anchor Sourabh Raaj Jain delves into the intricate details of Japan's earthquake preparedness, analyzing how the country continues to stand tall against these natural calamities.  Trending Photos NEW DELHI: The first day of the New Year 2024 witnessed Japan grappling with a major seismic event that reverberated across the globe. Simultaneously, Tokyo Airport showcased another dramatic scene – a plane engulfed in flames. Despite the formidable 7.6 magnitude earthquake on Monday, Japan's response defied catastrophic expectations. The drama unfolded at Tokyo's Haneda Airport, where a Japan Airlines plane caught fire colliding with a Coast Guard aircraft on the runway. The Coast Guard plane was on a mission to deliver essential supplies to areas affected by the quake. Gripping footage depicted the collision transforming the runway into a blazing inferno, leading to the swift destruction of the aircraft. Videos circulating in Japan's media capture the intense impact and explosions on both planes, providing a chilling account of the incident. Amid the tragedy, all 390 passengers and crew members on the burning plane were successfully evacuated. However, the Coast Guard plane witnessed the loss of five lives. An ongoing investigation seeks to unravel the intricacies behind this unfortunate event.    जापान में आए भूकंप में छिपे 'सबक' का विश्लेषण चीन-पाक-कनाडा पर जयशंकर के Direct Attack  देखिए #DNA LIVE Sourabh Raaj Jain के साथ#ZeeLive #ZeeNews #DNAWithSourabh #JapanEarthquake #China #Pakistan #SJaishankar #TruckDriversProtest@saurabhraajjain https://t.co/SanRbzJfpK — Zee News (@ZeeNews) January 2, 2024  Japan, known for the highest frequency of earthquakes globally, stands out not just for enduring these seismic events but for its unparalleled resilience. Despite facing history's most devastating earthquakes, Japan has not only stood strong but also crafted an exceptional defence system. A video capturing people at a tourist spot continuing their activities during a seismic event highlights Japan's fearless approach. The lack of panic and fear in the recorded reactions underscores Japan's remarkable preparedness, showcasing a calm demeanour even during a seismic jolt of 7.6 magnitude. Japan's extraordinary capacity to withstand major earthquakes is a testament to its robust defence mechanisms. Despite the challenges posed by seismic activities, the nation remains resolute. In tonight's edition of DNA, Zee News prime time anchor Sourabh Raaj Jain delves into the intricate details of Japan's earthquake preparedness, analyzing how the country continues to stand tall against these natural calamities. results.actual_text: El descubrimiento de lo que parece ser una cavidad gigante dentro de
results.plain_text: El descubrimiento de lo que parece ser una cavidad gigante dentro de la pirámide de Keops profundiza aún más el misterio que rodea a la Gran Pirámide de Guiza en Egipto.  No se sabe por qué la cavidad está allí o si contiene algo de valor ya que no es fácil acceder a ella.  Científicos japoneses y franceses hicieron el anuncio este jueves después de estudiar el famoso complejo arquitectónico durante dos años.  Para estudiar las pirámides utilizaron una técnica de imágenes basadas en rayos cósmicos que puede percibir los cambios de densidad dentro de las estructuras de piedra.  La Gran Pirámide (o Pirámide de Keops) fue construida durante el reinado del faraón Keops, entre 2509 a.C y 2483 a.C  Con una altura de 140 metros, es la más grande de las pirámides de Guiza, en las afueras de El Cairo.  Cavidad inesperada  La Pirámide de Keops contiene tres cámaras interiores grandes y una serie de pasajes. La más impresionante es la Gran Galería que tiene 47 metros de largo por 8 metros de ancho.  La cavidad recién identificada está directamente por encima de la galería y tiene dimensiones similares.  "No sabemos si se trata de un espacio horizontal o inclinado; no sabemos si está conformado por una o varias estructuras sucesivas", explicó Mehdi Tayoubi, del Instituto HIP de París.  "De lo que estamos seguros es de que este gran vacío está allí, que es impresionantes y que, por lo que sabemos, no había sido anticipado por ninguna teoría".  Importancia dudosa  El equipo ScanPyramids deliberadamente no utiliza la palabra cámara para describir este espacio.  Keops contiene compartimientos que los expertos creen que pudieron haber sido incorporados por los constructores para evitar que la estructura colapse, al aliviar la tensión provocada por el peso de las capas de roca superpuestas.  La cámara del rey (la más alta), por ejemplo, tiene por encima cinco espacios como estos.  El renombrado arqueólogo estadounidense Mark Lehner es parte del panel que revisa el trabajo de ScanPyramid.  Por qué es probable que no hayas oído hablar del Reino Aksum, una de las cuatro grandes civilizaciones del mundo antiguo En su opinión, la técnica de muones utilizada (unas partículas similares a los electrones) es sólida. Pero, aún así, no está convencido de la importancia del hallazgo.  "Podría ser un espacio que los constructores dejaron para proteger el techo muy angosto de la Gran Galería del peso de la pirámide", le dijo el experto a la BBC.  "Ahora mismo (…) es una anomalía. Pero necesitamos enfocarnos más en ello, especialmente en una época en que ya no podemos perforar la pirámide con dinamita como lo hizo el egiptólogo británico Howard Vyse a comienzos de la década de 1800".  Técnica bajo la lupa  Uno de los líderes del equipo, Hany Helal de la Universidad de El Cairo, cree que el hueco es demasiado grande para cumplir la función de aliviar la presión, pero entiende que esto sea un tema de debate entre los expertos.  "Estamos tratando de entender la estructura interna de la pirámide y de cómo fue construida", dijo Helal.  "Egiptólogos famosos, arqueólogos y arquitectos tiene algunas hipótesis. Lo que estamos haciendo nosotros es darles información. Son ellos los que tienen que decir si era algo que esperaban o no".  Gran parte de la incertidumbre proviene de la información imprecisa que obtuvieron con la técnica de rayos que en inglés se conoce como muography.  Este sistema no invasivo fue desarrollado en los últimos 50 años para estudiar el interior de estructuras tan disímiles como glaciares y volcanes. También se utilizó para investigar los reactores nucleares de Fukushima.  Esta tecnología utiliza una lluvia de partículas de alta energía que llegan a la superficie de la Tierra desde el espacio.  Cuando estos rayos cósmicos superrápidos chocan con las moléculas de aire, producen una serie de partículas incluyendo los muones.  Estos se mueven a una velocidad cercana a la velocidad de la luz y solo interactúan de forma débil con la materia. Por esta razón, cuando alcanzan la superficie, penetran profundamente en la roca.  Pero algunas de estas partículas son absorbidas y desviadas por los átomos en los minerales de las rocas, y si los detectores de muones se colocan en una región específica, pueden obtener una imagen clara de las anomalías en la densidad de esa región.  Robot volador  El equipo de ScanPyramids usó tres tipos de tecnología y todos coinciden en la posición y la escala del hueco.  Lo que resta es investigar en profundidad esta nueva estructura.  Una posibilidad es hacerlo perforando un agujero de unos tres centímetros por el que se pueda introducir un robot volador.  Pero este método deberá primero ser aprobado por las autoridades egipcias.
